text:
Convicted child rapist Steven van de Velde says he considered withdrawing from theOlympicsdue to the unpleasant experience his family faced over the course of his involvement in Paris.

The Dutchman’s presence in the men’s beach volleyball competition proved one of the most divisive talks points of thissummer’s Games given he was convicted for the rape of a 12-year-old English girl in 2016.

Sorry, this video isn't available any more.

Aged 19 at the time of the crime, Van de Velde served just 12 months of his four-year sentence and has since managed to rebuild his career in the sport, leading to his selection in the Netherlands’ Olympic squad.

Throughout the tournament, though, Van de Velde – competing alongside fellow countryman Matthew Immers – was subjected to boos and jeers and Dutch Olympic bosses repeatedly stood by the 30-year-old amid an onslaught of criticism.

Van de Velde’s participation in Paris was ultimately brought to an end by Brazil’s pairing of Evandro Oliveira and Arthur Diego Mariano Lanci, who defeated their Dutch counterparts in straight sets at the round-of-16 stage.

The controversial athlete largely remained tight-lipped in the French capital, refusing to face questioning in the mixed zone after matches. However, Van de Velde has broken his silence upon his return to the Netherlands.

Asked whether he had considered not competing in the Games, Van de Velde replied: ‘I definitely thought about it, yes.’

Van de Velde says he could not accept the invasion into his private life, which involved his loves ones getting ‘hurt’.

‘That’s when I broke down,’ Van de Velde added, referring to a British tabloid using a picture of his wife in a story.

‘I did something wrong, ten years ago. I have to accept that.

‘But hurting people around me – whether it’s Matthew, my wife, my child… that just goes too far for me. That’s definitely a moment where I thought, is this worth it?’

Michel Everaert, the general manager of Nevobo (Netherlands Volleyball), meanwhile, insisted the storm surrounding Van de Velde had been ‘dredged up’ by British media.

‘It was organised. They’ve dredged up the whole story, those English tabloids,’ Everaert said.

‘They didn’t get what they wanted in the first game and then they just went around trying to incite people. And then also via social media.

Everaert even claimed he had met individuals who had been ‘approached’ by British tabloids to hurl abuse and boo Van de Velde during the Netherlands’ matches.

Van de Velde’s playing partner, Immers, whopreviously pleaded with fans not to boo Van de Velde, claimed ‘things were shouted from all sides’ following their opening encounter.

‘That was the most intense,’ he said.

‘When I was standing in front of the microphone, things were shouted from all sides, also at me personally. I honestly didn’t like that.

‘But I think I did a good job and answered the questions calmly. Afterwards, I felt pretty good about it.’

title: Convicted rapist Steven van de Velde sobs as he reveals he considered quitting Olympics | Metro News
description: Convicted rapist Steven van de Velde broke down recounting his family's experience during the Olympics, revealing he considered quitting.
